data.title: Norwegian Cruise Line appoints five directors in deal with Elliott
data.description:
Norwegian Cruise Line Holdings said on Friday it had reached an agreement with Elliott Investment Management and appointed five new directors to its board in a major reshuffle.

Earlier this month, Elliott Investment Management, one of the biggest shareholders in the cruise operator, had said the cruise operator needed new directors after it reported lower profit and a disappointing outlook for 2026.

The new directors include Alex Cruz, former chief executive of British Airways and Kevin Lansberry, former chief financial officer of Disney Experiences.

"We believe the experience and credibility of this newly appointed board will help restore investor confidence and return the company to best-in-class financial performance," said Elliott Management partner John Pike.

Last month, the hedge fund, which manages $80 billion in assets, disclosed a 10% stake in the company and criticized the board's management choices over the last decade.

data.title: Senate Agrees to End Shutdown. What It Means for TSA and Airport Wait Times. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By George Glover, Janet H. Cho and Liz Moyer

The Senate agreed early Friday to end the shutdown for most of the Department of Homeland Security, which could end the travel chaos that has disrupted holidaymakers' Spring Break plans.

Just after 2 a.m., Republicans and Democrats unanimously agreed via a voice vote to fund all of the DHS except Immigration and Customs Enforcement and Border Patrol through the end of the current fiscal year.

The legislation now goes to the House, which was expected to hold a vote as soon as Friday.

Funding for the DHS lapsed for more than a month as Democrats sought to restrict money for ICE and introduce stricter rules for immigration-enforcement officials.

"While Republicans worked in good faith to try to reach agreement, Democrats remained intransigent and unreasonable with their list of demands," Sen. Susan Collins (R-Maine), Chair of the Senate Appropriations Committee, said on Friday.

The Senate agreed to fund DHS just hours after President Donald Trump said he would sign an order directing the Secretary of Homeland Security to immediately pay TSA workers, who have been working airport security checklines without pay since the partial government shutdown began in February.

Trump said in a social media post he was acting to address an emergency situation and to stop the chaos at American airports, which he blamed on Democrats. "It is not an easy thing to do, but I am going to do it!" he wrote.

As the shutdown wore on, TSA workers started calling in sick or quitting, leading to staff shortages and thus long lines at major airports. It being the heart of spring break travel season has only added to the pressure to end the shutdown.

Acting TSA Administrator Ha Nguyen McNeill told Congress on Wednesday that more than 61,000 TSA employees are required to work without pay during the now 41-day shutdown, and that more than 480 TSA officers have left since the shutdown began on Feb. 14. "If still shut down this Friday, we will have reached nearly $1 billion in missed paychecks," she said.

McNeill said the shutdown has also decreased the number of people interested in working for TSA, and that those they have been able to hire are required to undergo four to six months of training before they are certified to work at security checkpoints.

That could become another pressure point this summer, when the U.S. is hosting games for the 2026 FIFA World Cup. McNeill said that at this point newly hired TSA officers wouldn't be able to work until well after that. The sporting event is expected to draw millions of fans from June 11 to July 19.

Write to George Glover at george.glover@dowjones.com, Janet H. Cho at janet.cho@dowjones.com and Liz Moyer at liz.moyer@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Wheat up 1-2 cents, corn up 1-2 cents, soybeans up 1-4 cents
data.description:
The following are U.S. expectations for the resumption of grain and soy complex trading at the Chicago Board of Trade at 8:30 a.m. CDT (1330 GMT) on Friday:

WHEAT - Up 1 to 2 cents per bushel

  • Wheat futures mostly firmed, following crude oil higher, as investors monitored developments in the Middle East amid mixed signals from the U.S. and Iran about efforts to end the war.

  • U.S. President Donald Trump announced a new extension of his deadline for Iran to reopen the Strait of Hormuz or face the destruction of its energy plants, after Iran rejected his 15-point proposal to end the war he launched with Israel.

  • Iran - which has said it is not in talks with the U.S. - continued to retaliate against U.S. and Israeli strikes by hitting Israel and U.S. bases, striking Gulf states and effectively blocking Middle East fuel exports via the Strait of Hormuz.

  • Algeria’s state grains agency OAIC bought around 690,000 metric tons of milling wheat in an international tender that closed on Thursday, European traders said.

  • The European Commission forecast that usable production of common wheat, or soft wheat, would fall to 125.9 million metric tons in 2026/27 from 134.2 million this season.

  • CBOT May soft red winter wheat (WK26) was last up 2-1/2 cents at $6.07-1/2 per bushel. K.C. May hard red winter wheat (KWK26) was last up 4-1/2 cents at $6.31-1/4 a bushel and Minneapolis May spring wheat (MWEK26) was trading 1-1/2 cents higher at $6.46-1/2 per bushel.

CORN - Up 1 to 2 cents per bushel

  • Corn futures firmed, gaining strength from wheat and oil prices.

  • The Iran war has upended the planting intentions of U.S. farmers, resulting in fewer acres of corn and the lowest quantity of spring wheat planted since 1970 as rising fertilizer and fuel costs and low grain prices dim the outlook for profits, analysts said ahead of a U.S. government report due on Tuesday.

  • Dry conditions in the U.S. Plains, particularly in Nebraska, are expanding ahead of planting season and current drought maps are similar to those seen at this time of year in 2012, one of the most severe drought years in U.S. history. Traders noted weather conditions can quickly change and rain is forecast for the eastern Corn Belt going into next week.

  • However, some market analysts say if drought conditions do continue, problems could emerge related to irrigation and government-mandated restrictions on water use in some of these areas. Reduced access to irrigation water can impact crop yields.

  • Argentina said it will allow firms to voluntarily blend up to 15% bioethanol in gasoline, in a bid to curb the impact from higher oil prices.

  • CBOT May corn (CK26) was last up 2 cents at $4.69 per bushel.

SOYBEANS - Up 1 to 4 cents per bushel

  • Soybeans were higher, following grains and strength in the oil market.

  • Grain and oilseed prices have broadly tracked fluctuations in crude oil during the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran, underscoring the role of corn and soyoil in biofuels.

  • Traders are awaiting a possible announcement of revised U.S. biofuel targets at a White House event later today.

  • Soybean seedings this spring are expected to jump as some U.S. growers shift acres away from corn and wheat, which require more costly fertilizer, analysts said ahead of a U.S. government report due on Tuesday.

  • CBOT May soybeans (SK26) were last up 1/2 cent at $11.74-1/4 per bushel.

data.title: U.K. Short-Term Gilt Yields Surge as BOE Rate Hike Expectations Grow
data.description:
By Miriam Mukuru

Short-dated U.K. government borrowing costs have jumped as markets priced in the risk of aggressive interest-rate rises by the Bank of England.

High energy prices due to the Middle East war have stoked fears of possible rebound in U.K. inflation. This could force the Bank of England to increase interest rates even as the economy struggles.

Investors are fully pricing in three quarter-point interest-rate rises by the BOE in 2026, including an 87% chance of a rate increase in April, LSEG data showed. This is a remarkable shift from expectations for two interest-rate cuts in 2026 prior to the start of the Middle East war.

Yields on two-year U.K. government bonds, also known as gilts, rose 10 basis points to 4.600% on Friday, edging close to the 4.714% level, a near two-year high hit on Monday, Tradeweb data showed. The two-year yield is up by more than 100 basis points since the start of the Middle East war almost four weeks ago.

The speed of the rise in the two-year gilt yields has only been surpassed during the 2022 gilt crisis when the then-Prime Minister Liz Truss announced unfunded tax cuts, Derek Halpenny, MUFG's head of research for global markets EMEA, said in a note.

"The scale of increase in yield in the U.K. has been huge," he said.

U.K. inflation expectations are the main drivers of higher gilt yields currently.

"Short-term rate expectations have been shaped by rising inflation expectations, with markets expecting the Bank of England to react aggressively to the expected rise in CPI," Sanjay Raja, Deutsche Bank chief U.K. economist, said in a note.

Due to rate hike expectations, short-dated gilt yields are rising faster relative to long-dated gilt yields, causing the gap between them to tighten. The spread between two-year and 30-year gilt yields narrowed to last trade at 107 basis points, from 150bps prior to the war, Tradeweb data showed.

"Concerns about inflation and, as a consequence, central bank rate activity, appear to have eclipsed worries about the effects of rising geopolitical risks on the back of the U.S. conflict with Iran," Mitch Reznick, group head of fixed income at Federated Hermes, said in a note.

Yields on the benchmark ten-year gilt rose 11 basis points to last trade at 5.065%, approaching the near 18-year high of 5.118% hit this week.

Write to Miriam Mukuru at miriam.mukuru@wsj.com

data.title: Tradeweb Announces Date for First Quarter 2026 Financial Results
data.description:
NEW YORK--(BUSINESS WIRE)--March 27, 2026--

Tradeweb Markets Inc. , a global leader in electronic trading across asset classes, will release financial results for the first quarter of 2026 on Wednesday, April 29, 2026, at approximately 7:00 AM EDT.

In addition, Tradeweb will host a conference call for investors.

 
WHO:     Billy Hult, CEO 
         Sara Furber, CFO 
         Ashley Serrao, Head of Treasury, FP&A and IR 
 
WHAT:    A discussion of financial results for the first quarter of 2026 
         followed by a question-and-answer session 
 
WHEN:    Wednesday, April 29, 2026, at 9:30 AM EDT

A live webcast of the conference call, along with related presentation materials, will be available at https://investors.tradeweb.com/events-and-presentations.

  • To join the call via audio webcast, click here.
  • To join the call via phone, click here to register in advance. Registered participants will receive an email confirmation with a unique PIN to access the conference call.

An archived recording of the call will be available afterward at https://investors.tradeweb.com.

About Tradeweb Markets

Tradeweb Markets Inc. is a leading, global operator of electronic marketplaces for rates, credit, equities and money markets. Founded in 1996, Tradeweb provides access to markets, data and analytics, electronic trading, straight-through-processing and reporting for more than 50 products to clients in the institutional, wholesale, retail and corporates markets. Advanced technologies developed by Tradeweb enhance price discovery, order execution and trade workflows while allowing for greater scale and helping to reduce risks in client trading operations. Tradeweb serves more than 3,000 clients in more than 85 countries. On average, Tradeweb facilitated more than $2.6 trillion in notional value traded per day over the past four fiscal quarters. For more information, please go to www.tradeweb.com.

Forward-Looking Statements

This release contains forward-looking statements within the meaning of the federal securities laws. Statements related to, among other things, our outlook and future performance, the industry and markets in which we operate, our expectations, beliefs, plans, strategies, objectives, prospects and assumptions and future events are forward-looking statements.

We have based these forward-looking statements on our current expectations, assumptions, estimates and projections. While we believe these expectations, assumptions, estimates and projections are reasonable, such forward-looking statements are only predictions and involve known and unknown risks and uncertainties, many of which are beyond our control. These and other important factors, including those discussed under the heading "Risk Factors" in the documents of Tradeweb Markets Inc. on file with or furnished to the SEC, may cause our actual results, performance or achievements to differ materially from those expressed or implied by these forward-looking statements. Given these risks and uncertainties, you are cautioned not to place undue reliance on such forward-looking statements. The forward-looking statements contained in this release are not guarantees of future events or performance and future events, our actual results of operations, financial condition or liquidity, and the development of the industry and markets in which we operate, may differ materially from the forward-looking statements contained in this release. In addition, even if future events, our results of operations, financial condition or liquidity, and events in the industry and markets in which we operate, are consistent with the forward-looking statements contained in this release, they may not be predictive of events, results or developments in future periods.

Any forward-looking statement that we make in this release speaks only as of the date of such statement. data.title: Central Banks Unlikely to Meet Market Expectations of Rate Hikes — Market Talk
data.description:
0904 ET - Central banks are unlikely to meet market expectations for interest-rate rises, Pimco's Tiffany Wilding and Andrew Balls say in a note. Markets have switched to pricing in interest-rate risessince the start of the Middle East war a month ago, as soaring oil prices revived inflation risks. Money markets price in three quarter-point rate hikes in the U.K. and eurozone this year, and an over-50% chance of a rate hike in the U.S., LSEG data show. "We believe central banks are unlikely to match the market's recent repricing of policy rate expectations," Wilding and Balls say, adding that recessions risks are rising. Markets are already tightening financial conditions, they say. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0852 ET - Currencies of energy-importing countries should continue to suffer if the Middle East conflict drags on, Societe Generale's Kit Juckes says in a note. This could bring a sustained terms-of-trade shock for these currencies, he says. "We will only recommend buying energy-importers' currencies against their peers." For instance, SocGen still likes the Australian dollar and New Zealand dollar but would also consider buying them against the Japanese yen, euro or other currencies of energy-importing countries. The bank says the euro is at risk of trading in a lower range of $1.10-$1.14, compared to $1.14-$1.17 currently, if oil and gas prices remain at their current elevated levels in a few weeks' time. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0840 ET - The Norwegian krone is likely to remain supported as long as the Middle East conflict lasts, Societe Generale's Kit Juckes says in a note. The risk of interventions to stem the krone's rise is increasing but the Norges Bank doesn't have any room to cut interest rates with underlying inflation at 3%, he says. That means any falls in the krone against the euro would be an invitation to buy the currency and therefore short-lived, he says. Rising energy prices resulting from the conflict have strengthened the krone due to Norway's position as a major oil producer. Meanwhile, the Norges Bank on Thursday signalled it could raise rates at upcoming meetings. The euro rises 0.1% to 11.1819 krone. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0832 ET - Bitcoin falls to a two-and-a-half-week low as investors show skepticism over a resolution to the Iran war any time soon. President Trump extended a pause on strikes against Iranian energy infrastructure by 10 days. However, a WSJ report that the U.S. is looking to send more ground troops to the Middle East raises concerns about a possible escalation. "Digital assets are softer again, reinforcing the view that crypto is still trading as a risk asset rather than a defensive one," Saxo Bank analysts say in a note. An expiry in large bitcoin options Friday could increase short-term price swings as positions roll off and hedges are adjusted, they say. Bitcoin falls 3.3% to a low of $66,293, LSEG data show. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0758 ET - Yields on U.K. government bonds, or gilts, advance as markets price in the risk of high U.K. inflation due to rising energy costs, Deutsche Bank's Sanjay Raja says in a note. Rising inflation expectations have led investors to fully price in three quarter-point Bank of England interest-rate rises in 2026, LSEG data show. This is a big shift from pricing in two BOE interest rate cuts this year, prior to the Middle East war. Ten-year gilt yields are up 12 basis points to last trade at 5.070%, Tradeweb data show. U.K. 30-year gilt yields climb 7bps to last trade at 5.675%. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0756 ET - The Bank of Spain in its quarterly forecast has ramped up its inflation expectations due to the war in the Middle East. Spain's central bank now expects EU-harmonized inflation to average 3.0% this year and 2.5% in 2027, well above the 2.1% and 1.9% respectively it expected in December. Alongside oil-and-gas price increases, the bank also expects food prices to climb. But despite the upward revision, the bank says the latest government support measures to alleviate energy costs will mitigate the inflationary effect this year, though that will reverse in 2027. The bank also edged up its GDP growth expectation for 2026 to 2.3% from 2.2%, though downgraded 2027 growth to 1.7% from 1.9%. (edward.frankl@wsj.com)

0755 ET - The dollar could strengthen further if flows into safe-haven assets rise due to the Iran conflict, MUFG Bank's Derek Halpenny says in a note. "If we are about to enter a more pronounced period of risk-off with larger equity market falls we would expect the dollar to extend stronger." In this case, terms of trade and yield dynamics are unlikely to play much role in currency direction, he says. In a more severe scenario where Brent crude rises to a range of $120 to $160 per barrel from $110 currently and equity markets take an even bigger hit, the DXY dollar index could rise close to 105, he says. The DXY rises 0.1% to 100.037.(renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0754 ET - Sterling is likely to underperform if risk aversion escalates due to the Iran war, MUFG Bank's Derek Halpenny says in a note. "The history of sterling performance in periods of risk aversion is not good and added to that yield can turn from a positive to a negative as investors turn their focus to growth." The speed of the rise in two-year U.K. government bond yields is the fastest since the 2022 mini budget which delivered unfunded tax cuts and hit U.K. growth as financial conditions tightened, he says. Sterling falls 0.2% to $1.3309. The euro rises 0.1% to 0.8653 pounds. The two-year gilt yield reached 4.714% on Monday, its highest since May 2024, according to Tradeweb. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0750 ET - Markets are pricing in more than three rates hikes by the European Central Bank, but this seems overblown, says Carsten Brzeski at ING in a note. Messaging from the central bank appears to suggest that it is open to a range of different scenarios, depending on the direction of energy prices. "As long as the energy price shock remains broadly contained, including first‑round knock‑on effects, it's far from certain that the ECB will react at all," Brzeski says. Data through April is unlikely to move the needle. But if the war in the Middle East is still affecting energy prices by June, a rate hike is clearly possible. "However, this is not our base case, as we expect the Strait of Hormuz to reopen before then." (don.forbes@wsj.com)

0642 ET - The inflationary impact of the Middle East war will be stronger in the U.K. than in the eurozone, according to a panel of economists surveyed by the Centre for Macroeconomics and NIESR. Panelists favored a tighter Bank of England monetary policy, with several citing credibility as a contributing concern given that inflation has remained stubbornly above target. "A somewhat hawkish approach is now needed to regain credibility and demonstrate that the bank is committed to low and stable inflation," says LSE economist and panelist Thomas Sampson. The median expected inflationary impact from the war for the U.K. was an increase of 0.8 to 1.1 percentage points, while for the eurozone it was 0.4 to 0.7 percentage points, the survey showed. (edward.frankl@wsj.com)

0615 ET - The long-term yen-dollar chart highlights the risk that the Japanese government could lose control of the currency if it weakens to more than 160 against the dollar, says Jefferies's Christopher Wood. While that isn't Jefferies's base case, Wood says it shows the risk run by the BOJ's hyper-cautious approach to normalizing monetary policy. If the BOJ surprises the market by tightening, it thinks long-term JGB yields would fall. Such a move would also give greater confidence that the yen has bottomed and won't break the key 160 level, particularly if the BOJ signals that it forecasts the neutral rate at about 1.5%, implying three more 25bp rate hikes, says Wood. The yen slipped into the intervention danger zone in afternoon Asian trade as the dollar hit a 20-month high of 159.97. (fabiana.negrinochoa@wsj.com)

0543 ET - Japan's Finance Minister Satsuki Katayama has upped the rhetoric on possible currency interventions to stem the yen's weakness but words are losing value, MUFG Bank's Derek Halpenny says in a note. It's also unclear how quickly any interventions would take place if the dollar rises above 160 yen, he says. Given the fundamental backdrop where the Iran war is driving up the dollar, the Ministry of Finance could allow a more extended move than currently assumed, he says. "We would still expect intervention though and don't rule out U.S. involvement either," Halpenny says. The dollar trades flat at 159.81 yen after hitting a 20-month high of 159.97 earlier, LSEG data show. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

data.title: Russia will cut 2026 growth forecast, Interfax quotes economy minister as saying
data.description:
Russia will cut its official forecast for gross domestic product (GDP) growth for 2026, which is currently at 1.3%, Interfax news agency quoted Economy Minister Maxim Reshetnikov as saying on Friday.

Growth slowed sharply to 1% last year from 4.3% in 2024 mostly due to the central bank hiking its key interest rate to fight inflation. Low prices for Russian oil before the U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran contributed to the slowdown.

"We anticipated that the first half of the year would be challenging, and it turned out to be so. We will take all current indicators into account in the new forecast, which is currently being worked on," Reshetnikov was quoted as saying.

The Economy Ministry is expected to release the new set of economic forecasts in April. Reshetnikov did not specify the size of the planned cut in the GDP growth forecast.

He said further cuts in the central bank's key interest rate were unlikely to have a significant impact on the economic outlook for this year.

Reshetnikov added that a strong trade balance and capital flows would push the rouble higher and the ministry would review its exchange rate forecast accordingly.

data.title: Virtuix Delivers Omni One to U.S. Marine Corps for TECOM Evaluation
data.description:
Virtuix delivered an Omni One omni-directional treadmill to the U.S. Marine Corps for evaluation in warfighter training and mission planning.

Key Highlights:

  • TECOM, via partner KBR, purchased an Omni One for experimentation at the TECOM Integration Lab in Quantico.
  • System scheduled for delivery to Quantico in April 2026 for evaluation in training and mission planning.
  • Deployment expands Virtuix’s defense footprint following prior sales to West Point, the Air Force Academy, and Yokota Air Base.
  • Virtuix positions Virtual Terrain Walk to enhance realism in operational planning, mission rehearsal and combat readiness.

Original SEC Filing: Virtuix Holdings Inc. [ VTIX ] - 8-K - Mar. 27, 2026

data.title: Cibus launches underwritten offering of 6.98M shares at $2.15 to raise about $13.5M
data.description:
Cibus announced an underwritten public offering of 6,976,744 shares of Class A common stock at $2.15 per share.

Key Highlights:

  • Offering size: 6,976,744 shares at $2.15 per share with a 30-day option for 1,046,511 additional shares.
  • Estimated net proceeds: approximately $13.5 million, or $16.0 million if the option is fully exercised.
  • Underwriter: BTIG, LLC; underwriting discount of 7.0% and reimbursement of up to $150,000 in certain expenses.
  • Lock-up: company, directors and officers agreed not to sell Class A shares for 60 days, subject to exceptions.

Original SEC Filing: Cibus, Inc. [ CBUS ] - 8-K - Mar. 27, 2026

data.title: Top AI conference reverses ban on papers from US-sanctioned entities after Chinese boycott
data.description:
Key points:
  • NeurIPS conference said it had to comply with US law
  • Later apologised, reversed course after outrage in China
  • US and China competing for AI lead, talent

By Eduardo Baptista

A leading artificial intelligence conference on Friday reversed a policy change that would have banned papers from researchers at any entity under U.S. sanctions, soon after a boycott from China's largest federation for technology professionals.

The Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems, known as NeurIPS, published the new policy earlier this week, saying its California-based foundation had to comply with U.S. law.

NeurIPS - where AI companies and experts meet every December to present peer-reviewed research and discuss the latest breakthroughs - said on Friday the policy had been issued in error and apologised.

The rule change had marked an expansion of previous restrictions that only banned submissions from entities on the U.S. Treasury's Specially Designated Nationals List, a programme often used to target militants and drug traffickers.

The announcement sparked outrage in China, which is locked in an intensifying AI race with the United States, and has seen hundreds of its companies and universities put on U.S. trade blacklists.

The China Association for Science and Technology (CAST) said in a statement earlier on Friday it would stop accepting funding applications for members wanting to attend NeurIPS.

It said it would instead redirect them to domestic conferences or "international conferences that respect the rights and interests of Chinese academics".

Soon after, NeurIPS said on X that the new restrictions had been published by mistake because of a miscommunication with its legal team and that it had updated its policy to make clear that restrictions on submissions applied only to those on the SDN list.

"The responsibility for that error is ours as an organization, and we deeply apologize for the alarm and impact this miscommunication had on our community," NeurIPS said.
